beginner

Ollama: The Complete Guide

15 min read Updated May 2026

Table of Contents

  1. What is Ollama?
  2. Installation
  3. Running Your First Model
  4. Model Management
  5. Custom Modelfiles
  6. OpenAI-Compatible API
  7. Running Multiple Models
  8. GPU Acceleration
  9. Production Considerations
  10. Troubleshooting

What is Ollama?

Ollama is the simplest way to run large language models locally. It wraps llama.cpp under the hood, providing a clean CLI and an OpenAI-compatible API server. Think of it as Docker for LLMs — you pull a model by name and run it with one command.

Why Ollama? If you're new to local LLMs, start here. It handles model downloading, quantization, GPU detection, and API serving automatically. You can go from zero to a running model in under 2 minutes.

Installation

The one-liner install script handles everything:

curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh

After installation, verify it's running:

$ ollama --version
ollama version 0.5.12

$ systemctl status ollama
● ollama.service - Ollama Service
     Loaded: loaded (/etc/systemd/system/ollama.service; enabled; preset: enabled)
     Active: active (running) since Mon 2026-05-25 10:30:42 UTC
   Main PID: 3742 (ollama)
     Tasks: 18
     Memory: 89.2M
     CPU: 1.234s
     CGroup: /system.slice/ollama.service
             └─3742 /usr/local/bin/ollama serve

Ollama runs as a systemd service by default, making it available at http://localhost:11434. If you want to run it manually, or need a cloud server with GPU for training and experimentation:

ollama serve
# Logs: 2026/05/25 10:31:12 server.go:108: Listening on 127.0.0.1:11434

Docker Installation

Prefer containers? Ollama has an official Docker image:

docker run -d --name ollama \
  -v ollama:/root/.ollama \
  -p 11434:11434 \
  --gpus all \
  ollama/ollama

Running Your First Model

Let's pull and run Mistral, a solid 7B parameter model that runs on most hardware:

$ ollama pull mistral
pulling manifest
pulling 61e6e3b2c8cb... 100% ████████████████████████ 4.1 GB/4.1 GB
pulling e8a35b5937b7... 100% ████████████████████████ 8.0 KB/8.0 KB
pulling 6457a1f6025d... 100% ████████████████████████ 112 B/112 B
verifying sha256 digest
writing manifest
removing any unused layers
success

Now run it interactively:

$ ollama run mistral
>>> Write a Python function to check if a number is prime.

Here's a simple implementation:

def is_prime(n):
    if n < 2:
        return False
    for i in range(2, int(n ** 0.5) + 1):
        if n % i == 0:
            return False
    return True

The function handles edge cases (n < 2) and only checks divisors up to √n for efficiency.

>>> /exit

That's it. You just ran a 7B parameter LLM on your local machine.

Model Management

List all downloaded models:

$ ollama list
NAME                      ID              SIZE      MODIFIED
mistral:latest            61e6e3b2c8cb    4.1 GB    2 days ago
llama3.2:3b               2662b4f2ac4b    2.0 GB    5 days ago
llama3.1:8b               46e0c10c039e    4.7 GB    7 days ago
nomic-embed-text:latest   0a109f422b47    274 MB    3 days ago
codellama:7b              8fdf8f7adf5c    3.8 GB    6 hours ago

Remove models you no longer need:

ollama rm codellama:7b

Show a model's details:

$ ollama show mistral
  Model
    architecture        mistral
    parameters          7.2B
    quantization        Q4_K_M
    context length      32768
    embedding length    4096

  Parameters
    stop                "<|end_of_text|>"
    temperature         0.7

  License
    Apache 2.0

Custom Modelfiles

Modelfiles are Ollama's equivalent of a Dockerfile — they let you customize a model's behavior, system prompt, temperature, and more.

Create a Modelfile for a coding assistant:

FROM codellama:7b

# Set parameters
PARAMETER temperature 0.2
PARAMETER top_p 0.9
PARAMETER stop ""

# Custom system prompt
SYSTEM """You are an expert software engineer. Write clean, well-documented code.
Always explain your reasoning before showing code. Prefer Python unless asked otherwise.
If there are multiple valid approaches, mention the tradeoffs briefly."""

# Add custom template
TEMPLATE """{{ .System }}

User: {{ .Prompt }}

Assistant: Let me think through this carefully.
"""

Build and run it:

ollama create code-assistant -f Modelfile
ollama run code-assistant

Useful Parameter Combinations

Use CaseTemperatureTop-PTop-K
Code generation0.1 - 0.20.940
Creative writing0.7 - 0.90.9560
Factual Q&A0.3 - 0.50.840
Brainstorming0.8 - 1.01.0100

OpenAI-Compatible API

Ollama exposes an API that's compatible with the OpenAI format, so you can use any OpenAI client library with a local model.

Basic chat completion:

curl http://localhost:11434/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "model": "mistral",
    "messages": [
      {"role": "user", "content": "What is the derivative of sin(x)?"}
    ],
    "temperature": 0.3
  }'

Response:

{
  "id": "chatcmpl-abc123",
  "object": "chat.completion",
  "created": 1748219200,
  "model": "mistral",
  "choices": [{
    "index": 0,
    "message": {
      "role": "assistant",
      "content": "The derivative of sin(x) is cos(x).\n\nThis is one of the fundamental derivatives in calculus. Using the limit definition:\n\nd/dx sin(x) = lim[h→0] (sin(x+h) - sin(x))/h\n             = cos(x)"
    },
    "finish_reason": "stop"
  }],
  "usage": {
    "prompt_tokens": 36,
    "completion_tokens": 58,
    "total_tokens": 94
  }
}

You can use any OpenAI SDK — just change the base URL:

from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(
    base_url="http://localhost:11434/v1",
    api_key="ollama"  # Ollama doesn't require an API key
)

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="mistral",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Hello!"}]
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)

Running Multiple Models

Ollama keeps models loaded in memory until they're evicted. You can run different models for different tasks without restarting:

# In terminal 1: general chat
ollama run llama3.1:8b

# In terminal 2: code generation
ollama run codellama:7b

# In terminal 3: embeddings
curl http://localhost:11434/api/embeddings \
  -d '{"model": "nomic-embed-text", "prompt": "Hello world"}'

To see which models are currently loaded in memory:

$ ollama ps
NAME              ID              SIZE   PROCESSOR    UNTIL
llama3.1:8b       46e0c10c039e    5.5 GB 100% CPU     4 minutes from now
mistral:latest    61e6e3b2c8cb    4.1 GB 100% CPU     5 minutes from now

Models are automatically unloaded after 5 minutes of inactivity. You can adjust this with the OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE environment variable (set to 0 to never keep models loaded, or 24h to keep them loaded permanently).

GPU Acceleration

Ollama automatically detects and uses NVIDIA GPUs with CUDA. To check if GPU acceleration is working:

$ ollama run mistral
>>> Tell me if I'm running on GPU right now.

Yes! I can confirm I'm running on GPU. Ollama detected your NVIDIA GPU
and loaded the model with CUDA acceleration. You can verify this by
checking GPU utilization while I'm running:

$ nvidia-smi
Mon May 25 10:35:22 2026
+------------------------------------------------------+
| NVIDIA-SMI 555.42.06    Driver Version: 555.42.06    |
+------------------------------------------------------+
| GPU  Name        Persistence-M| Bus-Id        Disp.A |
|    0  RTX 4090              On | 00000000:01:00.0  On |
+------------------------------------------------------+
| 30%   48C    P0    185W / 450W |  7234MiB / 24564MiB |
+------------------------------------------------------+

>>> /exit

For AMD GPUs (ROCm) or Intel GPUs (oneAPI), install the Ollama ROCm or Intel-specific packages:

# AMD GPU
curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | OLLAMA_ROCM=1 sh

# Intel GPU
curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | OLLAMA_INTEL_GPU=1 sh

CPU only? You can still run 7B models comfortably on a modern CPU with 16GB+ RAM. Expect 2-5 tokens/second on a modern CPU versus 40-80 tokens/second on an RTX 4090.

Production Considerations

When running Ollama in production or as a service:

Environment Variables

# Increase context window (default: 2048)
OLLAMA_CONTEXT_LENGTH=8192

# Keep models loaded for 1 hour
OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=1h

# Limit concurrent requests
OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL=4

# Maximum queue size (beyond this, requests return 503)
OLLAMA_MAX_QUEUE=512

# Host on all interfaces (for containers)
OLLAMA_HOST=0.0.0.0:11434

# Set batch size for prompt processing (higher = faster, more memory)
OLLAMA_NUM_BATCH=512

Systemd Configuration

Override the service file to set environment variables:

sudo mkdir -p /etc/systemd/system/ollama.service.d

cat > /etc/systemd/system/ollama.service.d/override.conf << 'EOF'
[Service]
Environment="OLLAMA_CONTEXT_LENGTH=8192"
Environment="OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=1h"
Environment="OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL=4"
EOF

sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl restart ollama

Reverse Proxy (Nginx)

server {
    listen 80;
    server_name ollama.yourdomain.com;

    client_max_body_size 100M;

    location / {
        proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:11434;
        proxy_http_version 1.1;
        proxy_set_header Upgrade $http_upgrade;
        proxy_set_header Connection "upgrade";
        proxy_set_header Host $host;
        proxy_read_timeout 300s;
    }
}

Troubleshooting

Model Won't Load (OOM)

Your model is too large for your available memory. Solutions:

  • Use a smaller quant: ollama pull mistral:q3_k_s instead of the default Q4
  • Use a smaller model: try llama3.2:3b (2GB) instead of llama3.1:8b (4.7GB)
  • Close other applications to free RAM

Slow Response Times

  • Check if GPU is being utilized: ollama ps should show GPU in the Processor column
  • Reduce context length: OLLAMA_CONTEXT_LENGTH=4096
  • Increase batch size: OLLAMA_NUM_BATCH=1024

API Returns 503

The request queue is full. Increase max queue size or reduce concurrent requests:

export OLLAMA_MAX_QUEUE=512
export OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL=4

Connection Refused

Ollama may not be running. Check with systemctl status ollama and start it if needed:

sudo systemctl start ollama
